title: Neutropenic infections: a review of the French Febrile Aplasia Study Group trials in 608 febrile neutropenic patients
main_text: Introduction Ten years ago, a group of French haematologists and microbiologists formed the Febrile Aplasia Study Group (FASG) to further the understanding and treatment of neutropenic febrile patients. The Group's primary purpose was to produce a computer program for use by clinicians in the management of these patients. To achieve this target and draw up appropriate guidelines, the FASG aimed to compile a large database and document the results of several clinical trials of antibiotics. Between 1986 and 1992 the Group conducted several studies involving severely neutropenic patients. The criteria for patient inclusion remained unchanged throughout that period, thereby permitting a global analysis of the results of these studies. Each study sought to establish two points: whether standard doses of ceftazidime (3 g daily) could be used to treat febrile patients with prolonged neutropenia following chemotherapy/radiotherapy for haematological malignancies; and whether the antibiotic should be used alone, or in combination with an aminoglycoside 1,2 or a fluoroquinolone. 3 Given the increasing incidence of Grampositive infections, the Group also compared the efficacy of the reference combination of ceftazidime amikacin with that of piperacillin/tazobactam, a combination of a ureidopenicillin with a -lactamase inhibitor, amikacin. 4Patients and methods Between 1986 and 1992, 608 febrile, neutropenic patients were randomized to treatment as part of three consecutive study protocols that compared ceftazidime (1 g every 8 h) amikacin (7.5 mg/kg every 12 h) with several antibiotic regimens. During the first two study protocols, which were conducted between 1986 and 1990, a total of 386 febrile episodes were randomized to treatment as follows: 141 (of which 135 were evaluable for defervescence at 72 h) with ceftazidime amikacin, 98 with ceftazidime alone, 77 with ceftazidime vancomycin, and 70 (of which 64 were evaluable for defervescence at 72 h) with ceftazidime ciprofloxacin (Table I ). During the final protocol, which was conducted from 1991 to 1992, a total of 222 febrile episodes were randomized, 113 to treatment with ceftazidime amikacin and 109 with piperacillin/tazobactam (4 g/0.5 g every 8 h) amikacin (7.5 mg/kg every 12 h) (Table I ). Randomization was balanced at each of the participating centres. Sealed envelopes containing the antibiotic regimen to be administered were opened by the clinician or pharmacist, and the antibiotics were given unblinded. Patients could be randomized to treatment twice in Protocol I (ceftazidime, or ceftazidime vancomycin, or ceftazidime + amikacin), but not during the same neutropenic episode. In Protocols II and III, patients could only be randomized once. To be eligible for entry, patients had to be febrile, with a temperature of either 38.5°C for 3 h, or 38°C for 6 h. In addition to study medication, the only permitted prophylactic antibacterials were non-absorbable oral agents: colistin (1 MU qds) and gentamicin (30 mg qds vancomycin). Protocol I included vancomycin from the outset but, in the other protocols, vancomycin was added after 72 h in the case of persistent fever, or earlier if there was evidence of clinical deterioration. 5 Systemic antifungal therapy was also added if fever persisted after 48 h of vancomycin. Since all the trials involved adult patients with 0.5 10 9 polymorphonuclear neutrophils (PMNs)/L and an anticipated duration of neutropenia of at least 7 days, the data were pooled. The PMN trough was 0.1 10 9 /L in almost all patients. The mean age of the enrolled population was 46.2 years. Two-thirds of the patients had received chemotherapy for acute leukaemia and one-third had undergone a conditioning regimen of chemotherapy alone or in association with total body irradiation, followed by bone marrow transplantation for leukaemia, myeloma, lymphoma or solid tumours. The origin of the pyrexia was identifiable in approximately half the patients (septicaemia, 34.5%; local infection, 10.9%). The mean duration of neutropenia was consistent throughout the studies at 21 10 days. In addition, antifungal drug administration was the same in both arms of each of the studies.ResultsEfficacy of ceftazidime ( amikacin or vancomycin or ciprofloxacin) From 1986 to 1990, the antibiotic regimens administered during Protocols I and II (ceftazidime 3 g daily with or without amikacin or vancomycin or ciprofloxacin) resulted in the initial control of fever (apyrexia for at least 3 days) in approximately half the 297 evaluable patients (Table II ). 6 A comparison of the efficacy of the reference combination (ceftazidime 3 g daily amikacin) used in the three consecutive protocols revealed a significant decrease in the success rate between the first and final protocols (from 55% to 29%; P 0.0004) (Table II ). This loss of efficacy was attributed to an increase in Gram-positive strains responsible for initial infections; the ratio of Gramnegative to Gram-positive microorganisms decreased from 2.0 in 1987 to 0.7 in 1992 (Table III ). Fatal superinfections accounted for 8%, 1.5% and 7.6% of the infectious episodes included in the three protocols. Whereas there was no observable difference in infectionrelated mortality between the first and last protocols, the microorganisms responsible for death differed between studies. A three-fold increase in lethal disseminated aspergillosis (from 1.8% to 5.4%) outweighed the decrease in deaths from Gram-positive infections (from 3.6% to 0.9%).58Efficacy of piperacillin/tazobactam amikacin Two hundred and twenty-two patients were randomized to receive either piperacillin/tazobactam amikacin (n 109) or ceftazidime amikacin (n 113). Patient characteristics were similar in the two groups. Three patients were not included in the main efficacy analysis: one who did not receive amikacin, one who did not receive piperacillin/tazobactam and one who received only a single dose of ceftazidime amikacin. The intent-to-treat population, therefore, comprised 219 patients, 130 of whom were being treated with non-absorbable drugs, usually four doses of vancomycin, gentamicin or colistin per day, to decontaminate the digestive tract. Tables IV and V show the results recorded at 72 h together with the post-treatment evaluations. Fever was controlled more effectively in patients receiving piperacillin/tazobactam amikacin than in those receiving ceftazidime + amikacin: 48.1% and 28.8%, respectively; P 0.003. Vancomycin was prescribed before 72 h in a similar proportion of patients in each group (32.7% and 43.8%, respectively; difference not significant). The initial antibiotic regimen resulted in defervescence (complete success) in 19 patients (17.8%) in the piperacillin/ tazobactam amikacin group and in eight patients (7.1%) in the ceftazidime amikacin group (P 0.02) (Table V this group needed additional vancomycin than in the group treated with ceftazidime amikacin: 80 and 100 patients, respectively, during the neutropenic period (P 0.005). There was no significant difference, however, in the total antibiotic cost (P 0.25). Whereas the majority of patients in both groups experienced a second episode of fever, the total number of days with fever was lower in the piperacillin/tazobactam + amikacin group than in the ceftazidime amikacin group: 6.9 5 days compared with 9.2 8 days, respectively (Table IV ; P 0.001). Similarly, superinfections were less frequent in the piperacillin/tazobactam amikacin group than in the ceftazidime + amikacin group: 25 patients (23.4%) and 43 patients (38.4%), respectively; P 0.018 (Table V ). In the piperacillin/tazobactam amikacin group the following pathogens were isolated from blood samples or clinical sites in 16 patients: 15 Gram-negative organisms and four Gram-positive cocci (including five strains and one strain, respectively, resistant to piperacillin/ tazobactam), one Candida sp. and five Aspergillus spp. (Table VI ). It was not possible to identify the aetiology of infection in a further six patients who developed pneumonia. In the ceftazidime amikacin group, 13 Gram-negative organisms, 15 Gram-positive organisms (including seven and 11 strains, respectively, resistant to ceftazidime), eight Candida spp. and three Aspergillus spp. were isolated from 25 patients. The source of infection was unidentifiable in a further 14 patients, 12 with pneumonia and two with deep ear-nose-throat infections. There was no evidence of any difference between the initial treatment regimens in terms of mortality or major infectious events. There were 17 deaths resulting from infectious complications in the two groups. In the piperacillin/tazobactam amikacin group, death was associated with Aspergillus sp. (one patient), Pseudomonas sp. (one), Gram-positive cocci (not typed; one) and pneumonia of no identifiable causative organism (four). In the ceftazidime amikacin group, the causes of death were as follows: Aspergillus spp. (four patients), Candida spp. (two), Staphylococcus aureus (one) and undocumented pneumonia (three).60 Table IV Multivariate analysis revealed that there was a greater control of initial fever with piperacillin/tazobactam (P 0.005) and that a protected environment was important for treatment success. The ceftazidime amikacin regimen was more frequently associated with superinfection (P 0.018). A total of 105 organisms, 42 Gram-negative and 63 Gram-positive bacteria, were isolated from blood cultures before the administration of antibiotics. 6 In-vitro disc sensitivity tests were performed on 102 organisms to determine sensitivity to the two antibiotic regimens used. Fifty-eight (57%) microorganisms were sensitive to piperacillin, ceftazidime and the piperacillin/tazobactam combination (34/41 Gram-negative and 24/61 Grampositive bacteria) (Table VII ). In-vitro tests showed that ceftazidime was active against 72% of pre-treatment isolates taken from all patients (41/41 Gram-negative and 32/61 Gram-positive bacteria), and piperacillin/ tazobactam was active against 84% (40/41 Gram-negative and 46/61 Gram-positive bacteria). Of the 36 organisms that were piperacillin-resistant (seven Gram-negative and 29 Gram-positive bacteria), 56% were sensitive to the piperacillin/tazobactam combination (6/7 Gram-negative and 14/29 Gram-positive) (Table VII ). In Protocols I and II, toxicity associated with ceftazidime may have been responsible for three (2.7%) of the 16 reported cases of liver toxicity and eight (6.5%) of the 12 reported cases of skin rashes. In the final protocol, toxicity associated with piperacillin/tazobactam may have been responsible for six (5.6%) of the 12 reported cases of liver toxicity (one case of severe, grade 4 toxicity was attributed to a quinolone) and in 12 (11.2%) of the 15 reported cases of skin toxicity. There was no difference in toxicity between the two treatment groups, even for skin rashes possibly related to -lactams (P 0.07).Discussion This report presents the results of several studies involving patients with haematological malignancies who suffered profound and prolonged neutropenia ( 0.5 10 9 PMN/L) following chemotherapy. Fever occurred in 90% of such patients with 75% of the patients experiencing at least two episodes of fever during the period of aplasia. An empirical combination of a -lactam antibiotic and an aminoglycoside is recommended by some American 7 and European 8 authors as first-line therapy for febrile neutropenic patients. Despite an increase in the use of haematopoietic growth factors, febrile neutropenia often developed following autologous or allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantations. The increasing incidence of Gram-positive bacterial infections and fungal infections in these patients may be attributable to repeated courses of chemotherapy, the extensive use of indwelling catheters, and the long duration of neutropenia. Furthermore, patients with lymphoproliferative disorders are at greater risk of contracting aspergillosis as a result of high-dose corticosteroid treatment. Clearly, therefore, empirical treatment with an antibiotic targeted mainly at Gram-negative bacteria is not sufficient to protect the febrile neutropenic patient from infection during the neutropenic episode. This is demonstrated by the low frequency of 'complete success' (enduring defervescence without a change in antibiotic) observed in these successive trials. For the reasons stated above, the criteria used to evaluate antibiotics that are administered empirically to febrile neutropenic patients with haematological malignancies cannot be the same as for those administered for infections during neutropenia following chemotherapy for solid tumours. Any additions to, or changes in, the antibiotic regimen during the 3 week duration of neutropenia should not be considered as 'failures', but as 'treatment adaptations'. The major goal of antibiotic treatment is to avoid early mortality (essentially due to Gramnegative bacteria and streptococci) and to prevent severe superinfections or 'major infectious events'. 5 Successful treatment, therefore, whatever antibiotic modifications might be introduced during the neutropenic period, could be defined as a regimen that enabled a patient to survive the neutropenic episode and receive the next cycle of chemotherapy on schedule. Given the increasing incidence of Gram-positive infections, it is now of importance to administer empirically an antibiotic that is effective against these bacteria. Vancomycin, for example, may be administered from the start of treatment or within the first 72 h of treatment when there is persistent fever. During the first trial, fulminant streptococcal infections were prevalent in one of the two participating hospitals. As recommended, 9 however, vancomycin was added only in cases of documented infection, resulting in a number of early deaths related to streptococcal infections. 5 Where there is the risk of streptococcal infection, empirical treatment with vancomycin or an antibiotic known to be effective against the infecting Gram-positive strains, is essential. During the 1991-2 trial, patients treated with piperacillin/tazobactam amikacin experienced a higher frequency of defervescence at 72 h, a lower number of days with fever, and a smaller number of superinfections during antibiotic treatment. These results may be attributable to the broadened spectrum of activity provided by the -lactamase inhibitor. The increased incidence of sensitive microorganisms, confirmed by sensitivity tests in pretreatment samples, has previously been reported in comparative studies of piperacillin/tazobactam in nonneutropenic patients. 10, 11 Of relevance here are the findings of another study undertaken at the same time as the 1991-2 Protocol III and which used similar treatment combinations, but at higher dosages. Conducted by the European Organization for the Research and Treatment of Cancer (EORTC), 12 the study compared piperacillin/tazobactam 16/2 g daily amikacin 20 mg/kg daily vs ceftazidime 6 g daily amikacin 20 mg/kg daily in 706 cancer patients with post-chemotherapy neutropenia and fever. The combination of piperacillin/tazobactam amikacin was found to be significantly more effective (61% response) than the reference combination (ceftazidime amikacin, 54% response; P 0.05). Moreover, multivariate analysis showed that the probability of failure was significantly greater when patients received ceftazidime amikacin (P 0.02). Given that the combination of piperacillin/tazobactam is usually effective against potentially rapidly fatal streptococci, it may be possible to reduce the current wide use of vancomycin, which carries an increased risk of fungal superinfections. 13 Furthermore, if piperacillin/tazobactam were used empirically in neutropenic patients with fever, it might also be possible to limit the use of glycopeptides to infections related to identified Gram-positive organisms that are resistant to methicillin. The broad spectrum of action of the piperacillin/ tazobactam combination is somewhat at odds with the low incidence of complete success obtained in these studies with the first-line antibiotics. The reason for this apparent anomaly may lie in the long duration of neutropenia experienced by the patients in these studies. Recurrent fever was almost always present even when no organism could be isolated. The increasing frequency of fungal infections, which are not always identified before death, is currently a major problem in the management of patients with long-term aplasia. Disseminated aspergillosis is now the principal cause of death following infection during aplasia, especially in patients with haematological malignancies, as shown in our 1991-2 protocol. The threat of mortality during episodes of prolonged fever in the neutropenic patient justifies all preventive measures, including the reduction of environmental exposure, systemic prophylaxis 14 and the early use of intravenous antifungal agents.From 1986 to 1992 , 1992 the Febrile Aplasia Study Group conducted a series of studies involving severely neutropenic patients. The average duration of neutropenia was 21 days, following chemotherapy for leukaemia, or chemotherapy/radiotherapy as part of a conditioning regimen for autologous or allogeneic bone marrow transplantation. A total of 591 evaluable febrile episodes were randomized to treatment with either ceftazidime 3 g daily + amikacin (the reference regimen; n = 246), ceftazidime alone (n = 98), ceftazidime + vancomycin (n = 77), ceftazidime + ciprofloxacin (n = 64) or piperacillin/tazobactam + amikacin (n = 106). Only three patients treated with the reference dose of ceftazidime died or suffered serious morbidity from infections caused by Gram-negative bacteria. Piperacillin/tazobactam + amikacin was the only antibiotic regimen to have an effect significantly different from the reference regimen. Piperacillin/tazobactam + amikacin produced a higher rate of defervescence at 72 h (P = 0.003), fewer days of fever (P < 0.001), fewer superinfections (P = 0.018), a less frequent requirement for addition of vancomycin (P = 0.01) and a higher incidence of treatment judged to be a 'complete success' (enduring defervescence without a change in antibiotics) (P = 0.04). Despite the improved control of Gram-positive microorganisms, the infection-related death rate remained unchanged from 1987 to 1992. An increase in disseminated aspergillosis compensated for the reduction in lethal Gram-positive septicaemia.Table I .
